follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' '} |

[2026-07-04 23:49:52 INFO] flask app.py did not start for online log
[2026-07-04 23:49:52 INFO] Process design reviewer: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 3: Admission to Acute Care
This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation
2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides
recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the
prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be
considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum
prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the
definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8,
5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated
interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum
nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke
Network 2015
Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment
Module 3a Recommended Practices 
3.1 Stroke units
3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically
defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients
A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-
8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of
the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in
stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing,
physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.
• This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness
of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61):
Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke
unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome
of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in
institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients
admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined
outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a
nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency
3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care
professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational
therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and
clinical nutrition (a dietitian)
3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care
professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who
spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and
regularly complete education about stroke care
3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional
team 7 days a week
3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational
therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours
of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan
3.2 Early mobilization
3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should
be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),
unless contraindicated
Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24
hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be
reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time
frame and clinical judgement should be used.
3.3 Assessment
3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to
evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status
3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after
admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)
3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing
venous thromboembolism
3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged
for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism
3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be
assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening
tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,
dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.
Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for
recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for
patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements
3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or
discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;
OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 3a Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated
assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR
• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon
presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up
• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week
Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 3: Admission to Acute Care
This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation
2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides
recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the
prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be
considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum
prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the
definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8,
5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated
interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum
nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke
Network 2015
Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment
Module 3a Recommended Practices 
3.1 Stroke units
3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically
defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients
A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-
8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of
the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in
stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing,
physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.
• This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness
of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61):
Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke
unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome
of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in

admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined

professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational

3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care
professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who

3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional
team 7 days a week

therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours
of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan

3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should
be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),
unless contraindicated
Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24
hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be
reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time
frame and clinical judgement should be used.
3.3 Assessment
3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to
evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status
3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after
admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)
3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing
venous thromboembolism
3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged
for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism
3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be
assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening
tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,
dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.
Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for
recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for

3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke

Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;
OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 3a Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated
assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR
• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon
presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up
• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week
Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

  <activity role='ED clinician' action='confirm acute stroke diagnosis and admission indication' objects='clinical presentation and admission indication' id='activity1_1'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='determine applicability of the Module 3a acute care treatment pathway' objects='stroke versus TIA or minor nondisabling stroke decision' id='activity1_2'/>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='screen for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon presentation and document results for follow-up and TIA clinic communication when applicable' objects='screening results and EHR' id='activity2'/>

        </branch>
        <branch id='branch11'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway5'>
            <branch id='branch22'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='determine the time elapsed since stroke onset' objects='stroke onset time' id='activity17_1'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch23'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='assess contraindications to mobilization' objects='mobilization contraindication assessment' id='activity17_2'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway2'>
            <branch condition='24 to 48 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication' id='branch12'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='mobilize the patient early between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset' objects='early mobilization plan' id='activity18'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='within 24 hours of stroke onset and clinician judges mobilization reasonable' id='branch13'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='perform individualized cautious mobilization using clinical judgement and avoid frequent out-of-bed activity' objects='very early mobilization plan' id='activity19'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='mobilization is contraindicated or frequent very early out-of-bed activity is not appropriate' id='branch14'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='defer mobilization and reassess readiness while continuing prevention measures' objects='mobilization reassessment plan' id='activity20'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway3'>
        <branch condition='patient is admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='transfer the patient to in-hospital rehabilitation and communicate the acute care handoff plan' objects='rehabilitation transfer plan' id='activity21_0'/>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway6'>
            <branch id='branch28'>

            </branch>

                    <branch condition='the patient is unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements' id='branch26a'>

                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='no nutritional concerns or hydration deficits or dysphagia or other comorbidities were identified on assessment' id='branch25a'>
                  <activity role='clinical team' action='continue routine nutrition and hydration monitoring in the rehabilitation plan' objects='nutrition and hydration monitoring plan' id='activity27_1'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is discharged from acute care' id='branch16'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway7'>
            <branch id='branch31'>

            </branch>
            <branch id='branch32'>
              <activity role='interprofessional stroke team' action='complete or ensure completion of AlphaFIM on or by day 3 after admission and communicate results in the discharge or follow-up plan' objects='AlphaFIM assessment and discharge follow-up documentation' id='activity8_2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch33'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway4b'>
                <branch condition='nutritional concerns or hydration deficits or dysphagia or other comorbidities were identified on assessment' id='branch24b'>
                  <activity role='clinician' action='refer the patient to a dietitian within 7 days of admission for recommendations and communicate the referral in the discharge plan' objects='dietitian referral and discharge follow-up information' id='activity15_2'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway5b'>
                    <branch condition='the patient is unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements' id='branch26b'>
                      <activity role='dietitian and clinical team' action='consider enteral nutrition support and arrange the ongoing support plan after discharge' objects='enteral nutrition support plan' id='activity16_2'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='the patient is able to meet nutritional and fluid requirements' id='branch27b'>
                      <activity role='dietitian and clinical team' action='continue nutrition and hydration management according to recommendations after discharge' objects='dietitian recommendations and nutrition hydration plan' id='activity26_2'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='no nutritional concerns or hydration deficits or dysphagia or other comorbidities were identified on assessment' id='branch25b'>
                  <activity role='clinical team' action='continue routine nutrition and hydration monitoring through discharge instructions and follow-up' objects='nutrition and hydration monitoring plan' id='activity27_2'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='discharge the patient from acute care and communicate the discharge handoff plan' objects='acute care discharge plan' id='activity22_0'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient has TIA or minor nondisabling stroke' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='clinician' action='document that the acute care treatment recommendations in Module 3a are not applicable' objects='patient diagnosis and applicability decision' id='activity23'/>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='forward presentation screening results for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression to the TIA clinic for follow-up' objects='screening results and TIA clinic referral information' id='activity24'/>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='complete cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments' objects='Modules 5 and 10 assessment requirements' id='activity25_1'/>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='initiate and communicate cross-continuum secondary prevention therapies' objects='Modules 5 and 10 prevention interventions and follow-up plan' id='activity25_2'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
suggestion:
Problem: The AlphaFIM assessment is in the wrong order and is attached to the wrong gateway logic.

- In `exclusiveGateway3`, `activity8_1` is only executed in the branch `patient is admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation`, and `activity8_2` is only executed in the branch `patient is discharged from acute care`.
- This means AlphaFIM completion is made dependent on the later disposition decision, even though recommendation **3.3.2** requires AlphaFIM to be completed **on or by day 3 after admission** for acute stroke patients generally.

Suggested modification:
- Move the AlphaFIM activity out of `exclusiveGateway3`.
- Add a single AlphaFIM activity earlier in the acute inpatient pathway, preferably as another branch under `parallelGateway1`, because it is an assessment that should occur during acute care regardless of whether the patient later goes to rehabilitation or is discharged.
- After moving it, remove `activity8_1` and `activity8_2` from the rehab/discharge branches.

Example modification idea:
- Add under `parallelGateway1` a new branch such as:
  - `<branch id='newBranchAlphaFIM'>`
  - `<activity role='interprofessional stroke team' action='complete AlphaFIM on or by day 3 after admission and document results for ongoing care planning' objects='AlphaFIM assessment and EHR documentation' id='newActivityAlphaFIM'/>`
  - `</branch>`

Reason:
- AlphaFIM is a required acute care assessment for the inpatient stroke pathway, not a disposition-specific task.
- Keeping it inside `exclusiveGateway3` creates a logic problem because the rehabilitation/discharge decision may happen after the assessment deadline or may not yet be finalized by day 3.
- The current design also duplicates the same mandatory assessment in two mutually exclusive branches, when it should be a single common activity performed for all eligible acute stroke admissions.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care
professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who
spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and
regularly complete education about stroke care
3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional
team 7 days a week
3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational
therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours
of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan
3.2 Early mobilization
3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should
be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),
unless contraindicated
Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24
hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be
reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time
frame and clinical judgement should be used.
3.3 Assessment
3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to
evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status
3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after
admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)
3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing
venous thromboembolism
3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged
for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism
3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be
assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening
tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,
dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.
Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for
recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for
patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements
3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or
discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;
OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 3a Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated
assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR
• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon
presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up
• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week
Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

| Parameter | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **task_prompt** | Module 3: Admission to Acute Care This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation 2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke Network 2015 Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment Module 3a Recommended Practices  3.1 Stroke units 3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A- 8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”. • This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61): Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency 3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and clinical nutrition (a dietitian) 3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and regularly complete education about stroke care 3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional team 7 days a week 3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan 3.2 Early mobilization 3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset), unless contraindicated Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24 hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time frame and clinical judgement should be used. 3.3 Assessment 3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status 3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1) 3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing venous thromboembolism 3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism 3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits, dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian. Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements 3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 3a Implementation Considerations General considerations • All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR • Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up • Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack. |

| **phase_prompt** | Your task is to add calls to a Format Checking API to a piece of process text. You can call the API by writing 'check(text)' where 'text' is the process text you need to check for formatting. Module 3a provides\nrecommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the\nprevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be\nconsidered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum\nprevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the\ndefinition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8,\n5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated\ninterprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum\nnursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke\nNetwork 2015\nModule 3a: Acute Care Treatment\nModule 3a Recommended Practices \n3.1 Stroke units\n3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically\ndefined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients\nA geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-\n8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of\nthe time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in\nstroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing,\nphysiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.\n• This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness\nof Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61):\nModerate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke\nunit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome\nof death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in\ninstitutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients\nadmitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined\noutcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a\nnonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency\n3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care\nprofessionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational\ntherapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and\nclinical nutrition (a dietitian)\n3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care\nprofessionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who\nspend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and\nregularly complete education about stroke care\n3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional\nteam 7 days a week\n3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational\ntherapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours\nof admission to hospital and formulate a management plan\n3.2 Early mobilization\n3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should\nbe mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),\nunless contraindicated\nFrequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24\nhours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be\nreasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time\nframe and clinical judgement should be used.\n3.3 Assessment\n3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to\nevaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status\n3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after\nadmission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)\n3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing\nvenous thromboembolism\n3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged\nfor all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism\n3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be\nassessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening\ntool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,\ndysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.\nReferral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for\nrecommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for\npatients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements\n3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke\n3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies\n3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or\ndischarged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary\nprevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)\nAbbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke\nManagement; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;\nOSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.\n\nThe following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.\n\nModule 3a Implementation Considerations\nGeneral considerations\n• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated\nassessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR\n• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon\npresentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up\n• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week\nAbbreviations: ED, emergency department; When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n"} |

| **task_prompt** | Module 3: Admission to Acute Care This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation 2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke Network 2015 Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment Module 3a Recommended Practices  3.1 Stroke units 3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A- 8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”. • This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61): Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency 3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and clinical nutrition (a dietitian) 3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and regularly complete education about stroke care 3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional team 7 days a week 3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan 3.2 Early mobilization 3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset), unless contraindicated Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24 hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time frame and clinical judgement should be used. 3.3 Assessment 3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status 3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1) 3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing venous thromboembolism 3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism 3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits, dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian. Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements 3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 3a Implementation Considerations General considerations • All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR • Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up • Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack. |

[2026-07-04 23:50:24 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 3: Admission to Acute Care
This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation
2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides
recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the
prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be
considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum
prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the
definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8,
5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated
interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum
nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke
Network 2015
Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment
Module 3a Recommended Practices 
3.1 Stroke units
3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically
defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients
A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-
8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of
the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in
stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing,
physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.
• This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness
of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61):
Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke
unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome
of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in
institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients
admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined
outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a
nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency
3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care
professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational
therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and
clinical nutrition (a dietitian)
3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care
professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who
spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and
regularly complete education about stroke care
3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional
team 7 days a week
3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational
therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours
of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan
3.2 Early mobilization
3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should
be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),
unless contraindicated
Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24
hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be
reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time
frame and clinical judgement should be used.
3.3 Assessment
3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to
evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status
3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after
admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)
3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing
venous thromboembolism
3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged
for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism
3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be
assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening
tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,
dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.
Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for
recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for
patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements
3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or
discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;
OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 3a Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated
assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR
• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon
presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up
• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week
Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Your task is to add calls to a Format Checking API to a piece of process text. You can call the API by writing '<API>check(text)</API>' where 'text' is the process text you need to check for formatting. Here are an example of API calls:
Input: 
Please check the format of the process text:
<process>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='user' action='Alipay payment' objects='Commodity' id='activity3'/> 
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='user' action='Wechat payment' objects='Commodity' id='activity3'/> 
  </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
Output: 
Please check the format of the process text:
<API>check(
<process>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='user' action='Alipay payment' objects='Commodity' id='activity3'/>

[2026-07-04 23:50:49 INFO] Process design reviewer: **Process design reviewer<->Team leader on : AddAPI, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 3: Admission to Acute Care
This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation
2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides
recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the
prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be
considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum
prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the
definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8,
5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated
interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum
nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke
Network 2015
Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment
Module 3a Recommended Practices 
3.1 Stroke units
3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically
defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients
A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-
8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of
the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in
stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing,
physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.
• This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness
of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61):
Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke
unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome
of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in
institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients
admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined
outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a
nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency
3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care
professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational
therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and
clinical nutrition (a dietitian)
3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care
professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who
spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and
regularly complete education about stroke care
3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional
team 7 days a week
3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational
therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours
of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan
3.2 Early mobilization
3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should
be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),
unless contraindicated
Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24
hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be
reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time
frame and clinical judgement should be used.
3.3 Assessment
3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to
evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status
3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after
admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)
3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing
venous thromboembolism
3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged
for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism
3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be
assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening
tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,
dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.
Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for
recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for
patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements
3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or
discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;
OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 3a Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated
assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR
• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon
presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up
• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week
Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

              <activity role='interprofessional stroke team' action='evaluate stroke-related impairments using standardized valid assessment tools' objects='stroke impairment assessment tools and EHR' id='activity7_1'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch19'>
              <activity role='interprofessional stroke team' action='evaluate functional status using standardized valid assessment tools and document findings where possible in the EHR' objects='functional status assessment tools and EHR' id='activity7_2'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch3'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway2'>
            <branch id='branch4'>
              <activity role='physiotherapist' action='assess the patient within 48 hours of hospital admission and formulate a management plan' objects='physiotherapy assessment and management plan' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch5'>
              <activity role='occupational therapist' action='assess the patient within 48 hours of hospital admission and formulate a management plan' objects='occupational therapy assessment and management plan' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch6'>
              <activity role='speech-language pathologist' action='assess the patient within 48 hours of hospital admission and formulate a management plan' objects='speech-language pathology assessment and management plan' id='activity11'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch34'>
          <activity role='interprofessional stroke team' action='complete AlphaFIM on or by day 3 after admission and document results for ongoing care planning' objects='AlphaFIM assessment and EHR documentation' id='activity8'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch7'>
          <activity role='clinician' action='assess the patient for risk of developing venous thromboembolism' objects='venous thromboembolism risk assessment' id='activity12'/>
          <activity role='nursing staff' action='encourage adequate hydration to help prevent venous thromboembolism' objects='hydration plan' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch8'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway4'>
            <branch id='branch20'>
              <activity role='nursing staff and clinician' action='screen nutrition status within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid tool' objects='nutrition screening tool' id='activity14_1'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch21'>
              <activity role='nursing staff and clinician' action='screen hydration status within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid tool' objects='hydration screening tool' id='activity14_2'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch11'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway5'>
            <branch id='branch22'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='determine the time elapsed since stroke onset' objects='stroke onset time' id='activity17_1'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch23'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='assess contraindications to mobilization' objects='mobilization contraindication assessment' id='activity17_2'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway2'>
            <branch condition='24 to 48 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication' id='branch12'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='mobilize the patient early between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset' objects='early mobilization plan' id='activity18'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='within 24 hours of stroke onset and clinician judges mobilization reasonable' id='branch13'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='perform individualized cautious mobilization using clinical judgement and avoid frequent out-of-bed activity' objects='very early mobilization plan' id='activity19'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='mobilization is contraindicated or frequent very early out-of-bed activity is not appropriate' id='branch14'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='defer mobilization and reassess readiness while continuing prevention measures' objects='mobilization reassessment plan' id='activity20'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway3'>
        <branch condition='patient is admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='transfer the patient to in-hospital rehabilitation and communicate the acute care handoff plan' objects='rehabilitation transfer plan' id='activity21_0'/>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway6'>
            <branch id='branch28'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='complete cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments' objects='Modules 5 and 10 assessment requirements' id='activity21_1'/>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='initiate and communicate cross-continuum secondary prevention therapies' objects='Modules 5 and 10 prevention interventions and handoff plan' id='activity21_2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch30'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway4a'>
                <branch condition='nutritional concerns or hydration deficits or dysphagia or other comorbidities were identified on assessment' id='branch24a'>
                  <activity role='clinician' action='refer the patient to a dietitian within 7 days of admission for recommendations and communicate the referral in the rehabilitation handoff' objects='dietitian referral and rehabilitation handoff information' id='activity15_1'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway5a'>
                    <branch condition='the patient is unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements' id='branch26a'>
                      <activity role='dietitian and clinical team' action='consider enteral nutrition support and communicate the ongoing support plan to the rehabilitation team' objects='enteral nutrition support plan' id='activity16_1'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='the patient is able to meet nutritional and fluid requirements' id='branch27a'>
                      <activity role='dietitian and clinical team' action='continue nutrition and hydration management according to recommendations during rehabilitation transition' objects='dietitian recommendations and nutrition hydration plan' id='activity26_1'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='no nutritional concerns or hydration deficits or dysphagia or other comorbidities were identified on assessment' id='branch25a'>
                  <activity role='clinical team' action='continue routine nutrition and hydration monitoring in the rehabilitation plan' objects='nutrition and hydration monitoring plan' id='activity27_1'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is discharged from acute care' id='branch16'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway7'>
            <branch id='branch31'>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='complete cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments' objects='Modules 5 and 10 assessment requirements' id='activity22_1'/>
              <activity role='clinical team' action='initiate and communicate cross-continuum secondary prevention therapies' objects='Modules 5 and 10 prevention interventions and discharge plan' id='activity22_2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch33'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway4b'>
                <branch condition='nutritional concerns or hydration deficits or dysphagia or other comorbidities were identified on assessment' id='branch24b'>
                  <activity role='clinician' action='refer the patient to a dietitian within 7 days of admission for recommendations and communicate the referral in the discharge plan' objects='dietitian referral and discharge follow-up information' id='activity15_2'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway5b'>
                    <branch condition='the patient is unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements' id='branch26b'>
                      <activity role='dietitian and clinical team' action='consider enteral nutrition support and arrange the ongoing support plan after discharge' objects='enteral nutrition support plan' id='activity16_2'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='the patient is able to meet nutritional and fluid requirements' id='branch27b'>
                      <activity role='dietitian and clinical team' action='continue nutrition and hydration management according to recommendations after discharge' objects='dietitian recommendations and nutrition hydration plan' id='activity26_2'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='no nutritional concerns or hydration deficits or dysphagia or other comorbidities were identified on assessment' id='branch25b'>
                  <activity role='clinical team' action='continue routine nutrition and hydration monitoring through discharge instructions and follow-up' objects='nutrition and hydration monitoring plan' id='activity27_2'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='discharge the patient from acute care and communicate the discharge handoff plan' objects='acute care discharge plan' id='activity22_0'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient has TIA or minor nondisabling stroke' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='clinician' action='document that the acute care treatment recommendations in Module 3a are not applicable' objects='patient diagnosis and applicability decision' id='activity23'/>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='forward presentation screening results for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression to the TIA clinic for follow-up' objects='screening results and TIA clinic referral information' id='activity24'/>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='complete cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments' objects='Modules 5 and 10 assessment requirements' id='activity25_1'/>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='initiate and communicate cross-continuum secondary prevention therapies' objects='Modules 5 and 10 prevention interventions and follow-up plan' id='activity25_2'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>)</API>
